Main article: [Kupala Night](/wiki/Kupala_Night "Kupala Night")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Ivankupala.jpg/250px-Ivankupala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivankupala.jpg)

*Night on the Eve of Ivan Kupala*, by [Henryk Siemiradzki](/wiki/Henryk_Siemiradzki "Henryk Siemiradzki") (c. 1880)

[Ivan Kupala](/wiki/Ivan_Kupala_Day "Ivan Kupala Day") was the old Kyiv Rus' name for John the Baptist. Up to the present day, the Rus' Midsummer Night (or Ivan's Day) is known as one of the most expressive Kyiv Rus' folk and pagan holidays. Ivan Kupala Day is the day of summer solstice celebrated in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") on 23 June NS and 6 July OS. Before the day was named for St John, this was a celebration of a pagan fertility rite involving bathing in water. Since St John the Baptist's birth is celebrated at this time, some elements of Kupala's pagan origins were seen to be roughly synonymous with Christian meanings, most notably the parallel of Baptism as cleansing from sins, so the holiday in a Christian-modified form has been accepted into the [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") calendar.

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") from the 13th century, Midsummer was celebrated on Midsummer Eve (St. John's Eve, 23 June) and St. Peter's Eve (28 June) with the lighting of bonfires, feasting, and merrymaking.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: England")]

In late 14th-century England, [John Mirk](/wiki/John_Mirk "John Mirk") of [Lilleshall Abbey](/wiki/Lilleshall_Abbey "Lilleshall Abbey"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), gives the following description: "At first, men and women came to church with candles and other lights and prayed all night long. In the process of time, however, men left such devotion and used songs and dances and fell into lechery and gluttony turning the good, holy devotion into sin." The church fathers decided to put a stop to these practices and ordained that people should fast on the evening before, and thus turned waking into fasting.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Mirk adds that at the time of his writing, "...in worship of St John the Baptist, men stay up at night and make three kinds of fires: one is of clean bones and no wood and is called a "[bonnefyre](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire")"; another is of clean wood and no bones, and is called a *wakefyre*, because men stay awake by it all night; and the third is made of both bones and wood and is called, "St. John's fire" (Festial 182)." These traditions largely ended after the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), but persisted in rural areas up until the 19th century before petering out.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

Other Midsummer festivities had uneasy relations with the Reformed establishment. The [Chester Midsummer Watch Parade](/wiki/Chester_Midsummer_Watch_Parade "Chester Midsummer Watch Parade"), begun in 1498, was held at every Summer Solstice in years when the [Chester Mystery Plays](/wiki/Chester_Mystery_Plays "Chester Mystery Plays") were not performed. Despite the cancellation of the plays in 1575, the parade continued; in 1599, however, the Lord Mayor ordered that the parades be banned and the costumes destroyed. The parade was permanently banned in 1675.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Castle_an_Dinas_midsummer_bonfire_2009.jpg/250px-Castle_an_Dinas_midsummer_bonfire_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castle_an_Dinas_midsummer_bonfire_2009.jpg)

*Tansys Golowan* – A Cornish hilltop bonfire on Midsummer's eve.

Traditional Midsummer bonfires are still lit on some high hills in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") (see [Carn Brea](/wiki/Carn_Brea,_St_Just "Carn Brea, St Just") and [Castle an Dinas](/wiki/Castle_an_Dinas,_St._Columb_Major "Castle an Dinas, St. Columb Major") on [Castle Downs](/wiki/Castle_Downs,_Cornwall "Castle Downs, Cornwall")). This tradition was revived by the [Old Cornwall Society](/wiki/Old_Cornwall_Society "Old Cornwall Society") in the early 20th century. Bonfires in Cornwall were once common as part of *[Golowan](/wiki/Golowan_Festival "Golowan Festival")*, which is now celebrated at [Penzance](/wiki/Penzance "Penzance"), Cornwall. This week long festival normally starts on the Friday nearest St John's Day. *Golowan* lasts several days and culminates in Mazey Day. This is a revival of the Feast of St John (*Gol-Jowan*) with fireworks and bonfires. [Cornish wrestling](/wiki/Cornish_wrestling "Cornish wrestling") matches for prizes were often features of Cornish Midsummer Eve bonfire celebrations.[[86]](#cite_note-NE20061929-87)

In England, Midsummer Day (24 June) is traditionally one of the [quarter days](/wiki/Quarter_days "Quarter days").

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Midsummer festivals are celebrated throughout Scotland, notably in the [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") where [Peebles](/wiki/Peebles "Peebles") holds its [Beltane](/wiki/Beltane "Beltane") Week. The Eve of St. John has special magical significance and was used by [Sir Walter Scott](/wiki/Sir_Walter_Scott "Sir Walter Scott") as the title, and theme, for a pseudo-ballad poem. He invented a legend in which the lady of [Smailholm Tower](/wiki/Smailholm_Tower "Smailholm Tower"), near [Kelso](/wiki/Kelso,_Scottish_Borders "Kelso, Scottish Borders"), keeps vigil by the midnight fires three nights in a row (see above) and is visited by her lover; but when her husband returns from battle, she learns he slew that lover on the first night, and she has been entertained by a very physical ghost.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Wales")]

In [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), Midsummer is known as *Gŵyl Ifan* (English: [St John's](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") Festival), one of the ancient [Traditional festival days of Wales](/wiki/Traditional_festival_days_of_Wales "Traditional festival days of Wales"). The day was historically one of great agricultural fairs, with [Welsh music](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales") and [Welsh dance](/wiki/Welsh_dance "Welsh dance"). The *Nos Ifan yn yr haf* (St John's Festival Evening) would see Welsh people individually decorate their houses and keeping a communal bonfire alight throughout the evening. Other traditions include the washing of feet with [Mugwort](/wiki/Mugwort "Mugwort") (a plant believed to have been worn by John the Baptist in the wilderness) to ward off evil, and young maidens seeking [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") in hope of discovering who their future husbands would be.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)

Since the mid-twentieth century, a revival of Welsh folk traditions has seen Gŵyl Ifan become an important part of the cultural calendar. Modern Gŵyl Ifan celebrations are associated with [Welsh dance](/wiki/Welsh_dance "Welsh dance"), with events attracting musicians, dancers and performers from across Wales as well as inviting dancers from other countries and celebrating Non-Welsh traditions. Each year dancers entertain the public with [Traditional Welsh costume](/wiki/Traditional_Welsh_costume "Traditional Welsh costume"), ancient rituals and music. The *Cwmni Dawns Werin Caerdydd* (English: Cardiff Welsh folk-dance Society) perform the *Dawnsio Gwerin* (English: People's Dance) at the [National Museum of History](/wiki/St_Fagans_National_Museum_of_History "St Fagans National Museum of History") at [St Fagans](/wiki/St_Fagans "St Fagans").[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Solstice_fire_Montana.jpg/250px-Solstice_fire_Montana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solstice_fire_Montana.jpg)

Solstice fire in [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana")

Midsummer celebrations held throughout the United States are largely derived from the cultures of immigrants who arrived from various European nations since the 19th century. With the rise of earth-centered spirituality, many, including [Unitarian Universalists](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism"), celebrate the summer solstice as a religious holiday.

#### Alaska

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Alaska")]

As the northernmost state in the nation, Alaska straddles the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle"). Thus much of the state lacks civil [twilight](/wiki/Twilight "Twilight") from mid-May to Mid-July, engendering the nickname "Land of the Midnight Sun." Summer [solstice](/wiki/June_solstice "June solstice") is especially celebrated in the Interior Alaskan city of [Fairbanks](/wiki/Fairbanks,_Alaska "Fairbanks, Alaska"), with events such as the Midnight Sun Festival downtown, the [Midnight Sun Run](/wiki/Midnight_Sun_Run "Midnight Sun Run") from the University of Alaska Fairbanks to Pioneer Park, and the [Midnight Sun Game](/wiki/Midnight_Sun_Game "Midnight Sun Game"). The latter is an annual tradition in which a regulation game of baseball is played at 10:30 p.m. local time, through the midnight hour, with no artificial lighting, by the Alaska Goldpanners on Growden Field.

#### California

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: California")]

Since 1974, [Santa Barbara](/wiki/Santa_Barbara,_California "Santa Barbara, California") has hosted an annual Summer Solstice celebration, typically on the weekend of or the weekend after the actual solstice. It includes a festival and parade.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

In Santa Clara County, the Swedish American Patriotic League has held a Midsummer celebration at Sveadal for more than 120 years. It includes a parade, decorating and raising a maypole, dancing and other activities.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

#### Illinois

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Illinois")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Birgit_Ridderstedt%2C_sons_Lars_%26_Stefan_%26_TV_group_1959.jpg/250px-Birgit_Ridderstedt%2C_sons_Lars_%26_Stefan_%26_TV_group_1959.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birgit_Ridderstedt,_sons_Lars_%26_Stefan_%26_TV_group_1959.jpg)

[Birgit Ridderstedt](/wiki/Birgit_Ridderstedt "Birgit Ridderstedt") and suburban Chicagoland youths celebrate Midsummer on [WTTW](/wiki/WTTW "WTTW") television in 1958

In the [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois") area, Midsummer is celebrated in several locations. The historically Swedish neighborhood of [Andersonville](/wiki/Edgewater,_Chicago "Edgewater, Chicago") is prominent among them.[[92]](#cite_note-93) A stretch of Clark street is sectioned off for three days and there are vendors, live music, and a raising of the maypole. [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva,_Illinois "Geneva, Illinois") hosts a Swedish Day ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Svenskarnas Dag*) festival on the third Sunday of June. The event, featuring maypole-raising, dancing, and presentation of an authentic Viking ship, dates back to 1911.

#### Kansas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Kansas")]

[Lindsborg, Kansas](/wiki/Lindsborg,_Kansas "Lindsborg, Kansas"), commonly called Little Sweden, hosts an annual Midsummer celebration in honor of its 1869 Swedish founding.[[93]](#cite_note-94) The festival includes a 5K Run, free swim at the local pool, Kubb tournament, Swedish dancing, craft demonstrations, and food vendors, including the Lindsborg Swedish Folkdancers serving Viking-on-a-Stick.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The town is also the home of the [Swedish Pavilion](/wiki/Swedish_Pavilion "Swedish Pavilion") historic site, built as an international exposition building for the 1904 [St. Louis World's Fair](/wiki/St._Louis_World%27s_Fair "St. Louis World's Fair") and features a maypole year-round.

#### Maine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Maine")]

[New Sweden, Maine](/wiki/New_Sweden,_Maine "New Sweden, Maine"). Ever since 51 Swedish immigrants came to Aroostook County, Maine in 1870, supported by a Legislative Act authored by [William W. Thomas Jr.](/wiki/William_W._Thomas_Jr. "William W. Thomas Jr."), who had served as American Consul in Sweden under Abraham Lincoln, residents of the town have celebrated Midsommar. Celebrations are usually held the weekend before the holiday in Sweden and merge Swedish traditions such as the decorating and raising of the Midsommar pole, folk dancing, and feasting alongside American traditions. Thomas Jr. selected this region for a Swedish colony, in part, because the landscape, flora and fauna are similar to that of Dalarna County, Sweden.

#### Michigan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Michigan")]

In [Kaleva](/wiki/Kaleva,_Michigan "Kaleva, Michigan"), *Juhannus* is celebrated annually on or near the Summer Solstice by Gathering at the Village Roadside Park. Traditionally *Pannukakku* (Finnish Oven Baked Pancake) and strawberry shortcake is enjoyed followed by a bonfire or *kokko*. *Kaleva* was founded in 1900 by Finnish immigrants.

#### Oregon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Oregon")]

The Astoria Scandinavian Midsummer Festival has been a tradition on the North Coast of Oregon for over forty years. The Festival takes place typically on the 3rd full weekend of June. The festival embodies the rich cultural heritage that was transplanted to the [Astoria, Oregon](/wiki/Astoria,_Oregon "Astoria, Oregon"), region by emigrating Scandinavians. In the Pacific Northwest, they found the same bounteous seas and forests as in their native lands and the demand for their skills at managing them.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

#### New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: New York")]

The [NYC Swedish Midsummer](/wiki/NYC_Midsummer "NYC Midsummer") celebrations in [Battery Park](/wiki/The_Battery_(Manhattan) "The Battery (Manhattan)"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), attracts some 3,000–5,000 people annually, which makes it one of the largest celebrations after the ones held in [Leksand](/wiki/Leksand "Leksand") and at the [Skansen Park](/wiki/Skansen "Skansen") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"). [Sweden Day](/wiki/Sweden_Day "Sweden Day"), a Midsummer celebration which also honors Swedish heritage and history, has been held annually on the sound in [Throgs Neck](/wiki/Throgs_Neck "Throgs Neck") in New York since 1941. Swedish Midsummer is also celebrated in other places with large Swedish and Scandinavian populations, such as [Rockford](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"), [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis,_Minnesota "Minneapolis, Minnesota"), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), and [Lindsborg](/wiki/Lindsborg,_Kansas "Lindsborg, Kansas"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"). The Swedish "language village" (summer camp) *[Sjölunden](/wiki/Sj%C3%B6lunden "Sjölunden")*, run by [Concordia College](/wiki/Concordia_College,_Moorhead "Concordia College, Moorhead") in Minnesota, also celebrates Midsummer.

#### Washington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Washington")]

The [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington") neighborhood of [Fremont](/wiki/Fremont,_Seattle,_Washington "Fremont, Seattle, Washington") puts on a large [Summer Solstice Parade and Pageant](/wiki/Summer_Solstice_Parade_and_Pageant "Summer Solstice Parade and Pageant"), which for many years has controversially included [painted naked cyclists](/wiki/Solstice_Cyclists "Solstice Cyclists"). In St. Edwards Park in [Kenmore](/wiki/Kenmore,_Washington "Kenmore, Washington"), the [Skandia Folkdance Society](/w/index.php?title=Skandia_Folkdance_Society&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skandia Folkdance Society (page does not exist)") hosts *Midsommarfest*, which includes a Scandinavian solstice pole.

#### Wyoming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Midsummer&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Wyoming")]

A solstitial celebration is held on [Casper Mountain](/wiki/Casper_Mountain "Casper Mountain") at Crimson Dawn park. Crimson Dawn is known in the area for the great stories of mythical creatures and people that live on Casper Mountain. The celebration is attended by many people from the community, and from around the country. A large bonfire is held and all are invited to throw a handful of red soil into the fire in hopes that they get their wish granted.[[96]](#cite_note-97)
